text: Embedding a large-scale knowledge graph into a continuous vector space has drawn much attention in recent years. A recently proposed model, called TransE, achieved both high accuracy and efficient embedding. In this paper, we discuss several important relation mapping properties that need to be taken into account, such as the reflexive, one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-one mapping relations. Unfortunately, however, the TransE model performs poorly in these relations. While more complex models have been proposed to meet these requirements, they tend to sacrifice efficiency. Therefore, to balance the model capacity and efficiency, in this paper, we propose the TransH model by modeling each relation as a hyperplane with a translation vector. The TransH model can effectively preserve the relation mapping properties and its complexity is almost the same as that of the TransE model. In addition, we propose a strategy for constructing negative instances, so as to decrease the amount of false negative samples in the training. Using the one-to-many or many-to-one relation mapping properties of a relation, we can propose a very simple method to minimize false negative samples. Experimental results are provided on link prediction, triplet classification and fact extraction tasks on several popular benchmarks such as WordNet and Freebase. Experimental results show that TransH performs much better than the TransE model, and can also handle large knowledge graphs.
explanation: Coherent, domain-specific abstract with accurate references to TransE/TransH and common benchmarks (WordNet, Freebase). The phrasing is concise and lacks unusual AI artifacts, suggesting human authorship, though a high-quality AI paraphrase could not be ruled out.
